text: The surgeon performs an infiltration with local anesthetic and vasoconstrictor, aiming to achieve a bloodless operative field.
The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor after making a skin incision to open the wound. Further dissection is performed using scissors or a cold scalpel to minimize tissue damage.
The surgeon identifies and gently mobilizes the edges of the torn sphincter, carefully avoiding cutting the scar tissue and ensuring the anoderm and vaginal mucosa remain undamaged.

The surgeon mobilizes and blocks the edges of the sphincter without separately identifying the internal and external sphincters. The fibrotic tissue is left intact, and the mobilization extends up to the levator muscle if necessary.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the sphincter, taking care to avoid damaging the anal nerves located at the three and nine o'clock positions. The muscle edges are mobilized sufficiently to overlap by at least two centimeters.
The surgeon begins muscle suturing by placing overlapping U-stitches using absorbable monofilament, carefully tying knots to prevent ischemic damage to the muscle tissue.
Levatorplasty might be added if needed.
The surgeon closes the skin wound vertically to enlarge the newly created perineal body, ensuring the incision is sealed without the need for drainage.
